governance.label: Structured Criminal Monopoly
governance.description: Most regulated market on the border. City divided into gang-controlled territories, each running 'conectas' (sales points). Standardized doses, sealed packages with distinctive stamps, uniform prices citywide. M30 pills banned. Smoking pipes controlled by distributors.

fentanyl_market.method: Mixed with black tar heroin ('goma negra')
formats: Primarily fentanyl mixed with black tar heroin
fentanyl_market.regulation_description: Highly structured criminal monopoly with strict rules on form, price, packaging, and distribution

card_content.market_summary: Fentanyl is fully integrated into the heroin market and is widely consumed as an additive. The market is highly organized, with standardized doses and strict cartel oversight.
card_content.toll_summary: Overdose rates are significant, with paramedics reporting at least five overdose cases per week. Early introduction caused severe health impacts and ongoing overdose risks remain high.
card_content.key_insight: Most regulated drug market on the border — standardized doses, sealed packages, uniform prices. Even smoking pipes are controlled.

governance.label: Violent Criminal Oligopoly
governance.description: Multiple factions divide the city into zones with invisible borders. Drug packages are sealed with distinctive colors per criminal group. Extremely violent — ~70% of homicides linked to drug market rule enforcement.

fentanyl_market.method: Covertly mixed with heroin as white powder
fentanyl_market.regulation_description: Violent oligopoly with multiple competing groups, strict territorial control but more product variety

card_content.market_summary: Tijuana has a highly developed and diversified fentanyl market with multiple forms available. Competition between cartels creates both product variety and extreme violence.
card_content.toll_summary: Overdose levels are severe, with around three overdoses per day reported. The fentanyl crisis has caused widespread deaths and ongoing public health strain.
card_content.key_insight: ~70% of homicides tied to drug market rules. Crossing invisible faction borders risks death.

governance.label: Fragile Criminal Truce
governance.description: Distribution controlled through 'plazas' in different neighborhoods. Standardized doses and sealed packages. Both factions maintained a 'pax narca' for decades — fentanyl and heroin restricted to avoid 'heating up the plaza'. Truce fractured in late 2024, increasing violence and disrupting fentanyl supply.

fentanyl_market.method: Initially as heroin adulterant ('China White'), then independent product
formats: Heroin mixed with fentanyl ('China White' style)
fentanyl_market.consumption_method: primarily injection (smoking pipes controlled by organized crime)
fentanyl_market.market_openness: clandestine — closed networks connecting consumers to Sinaloa-based producers
fentanyl_market.regulation_description: Discreet, tightly controlled market, historically restricted to avoid 'heating the plaza', recently more unstable due to cartel conflict

card_content.market_summary: Fentanyl in Hermosillo is low-profile and distributed through closed networks rather than open street markets. Availability is sporadic and shaped by cartel control and recent violence disrupting supply.
card_content.toll_summary: Data is limited, but users report multiple overdose deaths within their networks. Hospitals report 2–3 fentanyl-related emergencies per month, indicating a smaller but still present health impact.
card_content.key_insight: Decades-long 'pax narca' kept fentanyl underground. The truce shattered in late 2024.

governance.label: Clandestine Market Under Criminal Duopoly
groups: Sinaloa Cartel factions — Chapitos / Mayiza (east side)
governance.description: Railroad tracks divide the city into two criminal zones. Salazar control meth on the west; Sinaloa factions control cocaine, marijuana, heroin, benzos on the east. Both factions restrict fentanyl sales — distributors who violate rules have been killed or disappeared. Fentanyl pushed to clandestine underground market.

fentanyl_market.method: Offered openly as M30 pills during heroin shortage
fentanyl_market.market_openness: clandestine — small distributor networks with trusted consumers
fentanyl_market.regulation_description: Highly restricted and clandestine, severe punishment (including death/disappearance) for unauthorized sales

card_content.market_summary: Fentanyl exists in a hidden, closed-network market due to strict cartel restrictions. Distribution occurs privately among trusted users rather than through open drug points.
card_content.toll_summary: Users report knowing many overdose deaths, though official data is scarce due to the clandestine nature of the market. Hospitals handle 6–9 fentanyl-related emergencies per week, suggesting significant but underreported harm.
card_content.key_insight: Railroad tracks split the city between two criminal empires. Wrong substance on the wrong side means disappearance.

governance.label: Total Criminal Prohibition
groups: La Línea / La Empresa (Juárez Cartel factions)
governance.description: City divided between Juárez Cartel remnants (heroin from Chihuahua sierra) and Sinaloa-linked gangs (cocaine, meth). Fentanyl actively prohibited by La Línea — selling it signals links to rival Sinaloa networks. Users and sellers threatened with death. Despite massive fentanyl trafficking through the Juárez-El Paso corridor to the U.S., the local market remains largely untouched.

fentanyl_market.method: Sporadic — offered covertly by international traffickers, mixed with heroin or as M30 pills
fentanyl_market.market_openness: effectively banned — admitting consumption carries lethal risk
fentanyl_market.regulation_description: Near-total prohibition with active resistance and threats of violence for use/sale

card_content.market_summary: Fentanyl is not integrated into the local market and appears only sporadically. Strong cartel resistance has preserved heroin dominance and limited fentanyl spread.
card_content.toll_summary: Fentanyl-related harm is lower but still present, including overdose cases and isolated deaths. A 2025 incident involving fentanyl-laced cocaine caused multiple deaths, showing ongoing risk despite restrictions.
card_content.key_insight: Fentanyl is banned by local cartels despite massive trafficking through Juárez–El Paso. Price: $10/dose — 4× the border average.
